findings.description: slugify can return an empty string for all-non-ASCII (e.g. CJK) or all-punctuation input, with no fallback, breaking URL routes / filenames / unique keys that assume a non-empty slug.

indent_block uses splitlines()+join('
'), silently dropping a trailing newline and normalizing all line endings (
 -> 
), corrupting round-trip content for a supposedly format-preserving helper.

mask_middle with default visible=4 exposes most of medium-length values (e.g. 9-char value -> '1234*6789', 8 of 9 chars visible), contradicting the docstring promise to 'avoid leaking most of the content'; threshold visible*2 is too permissive.
mask_middle returns mask_char * len(value) for short values, leaking the exact length of the masked secret (minor info disclosure for a masking helper) — defer detail to critic-security.

truncate with a multi-char suffix whose length >= limit (or default '…' with limit==1) discards ALL source text and returns only the (truncated) suffix, yielding contentless output like '...' — defined but degenerate, arguably unintended.
slugify silently returns '' when max_length <= 0 with no guard, inconsistent with truncate() which explicitly guards limit <= 0; a caller passing 0/negative gets empty rather than an error.
indent_block does not validate spaces; a negative value makes ' ' * spaces == '' and silently produces no indentation instead of raising, unlike mask_middle which guards visible < 0.

No public function (collapse_whitespace, truncate, etc.) validates non-str/None input; for a web-service entry point, None propagates to a raw TypeError at len()/regex rather than a domain error — consistent with type hints so borderline.

levenshtein has no input-length guard; O(len(a)*len(b)) time and memory on attacker-controlled strings is a DoS vector (two 100k strings -> ~1e10 ops) — algorithm itself is correct; defer detail to critic-performance.
